mechanism_narrative: Rising industrial production signals expanding real output, which boosts corporate earnings expectations and drives positive S&P 500 returns over a one-month horizon. The mechanism is always active as INDPRO is a coincident indicator of economic activity.
failure_condition: The 20-day forward return after INDPRO monthly increases is not significantly positive (one-sided t-test p > 0.10) over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate rises above a low threshold (0.25%), it signals the end of an accommodative policy stance. Market expectations of tightening cause discount rates to rise, compressing equity valuations and leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the following 20 trading days.
failure_condition: In months following trigger activation, the median 20-day SPX return is not negative or the proportion of negative returns is ≤ 50% over a 3-year backtest.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous strong payroll growth and falling unemployment indicate a tightening labor market that can generate wage inflation. This raises expectations of Fed tightening, negatively impacting equity prices. The joint behavior of PAYEMS and UNRATE is necessary for the signal; each alone is insufficient.
candidate_trigger: PAYEMS monthly change > 200k and UNRATE month-over-month decline > 0.1pp
failure_condition: In quarters following trigger, the average 60-day forward SPX return is not negative or is not significantly different from non-trigger periods (two-sample t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: In a low inflation environment (core CPI YoY < 2%), rising retail sales signal robust consumer demand without imminent overheating. This supports earnings growth and leads to positive S&P 500 returns. In high inflation periods, the same signal would be negative due to rate hike expectations.
failure_condition: In low inflation periods, the correlation between RSAFS monthly growth and 20-day forward SPX return is not positive (rank IC < 0.05) over any 3-year window.

mechanism_narrative: Increasing housing starts indicate future construction activity and residential investment, a leading component of aggregate demand. This forward-looking signal lifts GDP expectations and supports equity prices over a quarterly horizon. The mechanism works in all economic regimes.
failure_condition: The 60-day forward return following HOUST monthly increases is not positive in at least 60% of observations over a 10-year out-of-sample period.

mechanism_narrative: Prolonged extremely low federal funds rates (below 0.10%) signal that central bank policy remains aggressively accommodative. This depresses risk-free yields, pushing investors into equities for yield, boosting S&P 500 returns over a one-week horizon as liquidity effects are rapid.
failure_condition: In the 5 trading days following trigger months, the average SPX return is not positive or the win rate is below 55% over a 5-year history.

mechanism_narrative: When industrial production grows and the federal funds rate remains low, the environment is favorable for equity valuations: real output expands while discount rates stay compressed. The interaction of these two conditions amplifies the positive effect beyond either alone. Both proxies must be observed jointly.
regime_hint: accommodative monetary policy with solid growth
failure_condition: In months satisfying the trigger condition, the mean 20-day SPX return is not higher than the unconditional mean (one-sided test p > 0.10) over a 10-year period.

mechanism_narrative: In a high unemployment regime (UNRATE > 6%), rising consumer prices are interpreted as a sign of economic recovery rather than imminent overheating, because labor slack limits wage-push inflation. Consequently, CPI increases boost confidence and equity prices. The direction flips to negative in low unemployment regimes.
failure_condition: In high unemployment periods, the 20-day forward return following CPI increases is not positive more than 60% of the time, or the average return is not significantly above zero (t-test p > 0.05).
